facade_colour: brown-clad and cream-trimmed altered semi-detached facade

hcd_data.architectural_style: House-form, Semi-detached, Bay-and-Gable (16 Denison Square) (confirmed – HCD Plan Vol. 2)
hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as a House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian and early-twentieth-century architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Each property retains original building features including a cross-gabled roofline, double-height bay, and fenestration pattern. 16 Denison Square has retained its decorative wooden gable brackets while 18 Denison Square has retained its segmentally arched window openings with brick voussoirs and cladding. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. Each property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.

photo_observations.facade_condition_notes: upper front gable and wall plane are reclad; original massing survives but most visible heritage fabric is covered
photo_observations.window_details: replacement windows with simple trim; lower facade partially obscured by neighbouring porch line
photo_observations.decorative_details: front gable shape remains the primary historic cue
photo_observations.roof_type_observed: front gable with small dormer-like projection
photo_observations.alterations_visible: modern cladding, replacement windows, simplified front
photo_observations.notes: Observed in the paired 16-18 Denison photo.

facade_description: 18 Denison Sq presents a bay-and-gable domestic facade within the Kensington Market HCD. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its semi-detached bay-and-gable house-form typology. The facade reads primarily as mixed in a brown-clad and cream-trimmed altered semi-detached facade palette, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 2, 2 window bays from lower to upper levels, using mixed, and 1 principal entrance. Character-defining facade elements include bay window, original windows, cornice. HCD note: Each property has value as a House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian and early-twentieth-century architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Each property retains original building features including a cross-gabled roofline, double-height bay, and fenestration pattern. 16 Denison Square has retained its decorative wooden gable brackets while 18 Denison Square has retained its segmentally arched window openings with brick voussoirs and cladding. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. Each property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.
